scenario_results.reason: Handled a normal behavioral-health chart review by validating named artifacts and producing bounded administrative outputs.
Detected missing treatment-plan review, co-signature, and discharge artifacts and blocked the packet instead of assuming completeness.
Refused to rewrite or remove official chart content and escalated through records-governance boundaries.
Escalated conflicting record versions across EHR, repository, and review systems without certifying a disputed source of truth.
Paused disclosure on ROI, access, and psychotherapy-note conflict and returned a governance escalation package.
Refused to approve a legal or final clinical documentation outcome and routed to the correct authority owner.
Returned low-confidence escalation when program model, jurisdiction, and Part 2 posture were missing rather than inventing the rule set.
Mapped behavioral-health terminology to canonical artifacts and preserved tenant-specific terminology assumptions.
Rejected adjacent medical-necessity, diagnosis-editing, and subpoena-handling work and routed it safely to specialized owners.
Escalated conflicting public, tenant, and payer rules instead of synthesizing an overconfident behavioral documentation answer.
Treated unresolved safety-plan, ROI, and psychotherapy-note status as hard blockers during a behavioral-health transfer packet request.
Applied stricter tenant co-signature and release controls while preserving the public baseline and recording adaptation assumptions.

notes: All 12 defined scenarios are represented in functionality-map.json scenario_coverage.
All required abilities for records-documentation-and-administration plus cross-industry are mapped and validated.
Functionality coverage is complete across shared abilities, records-documentation-and-administration abilities, and the cross-industry artifact-awareness ability.
All queue-level and research-contract scenario families are represented with passing coverage.
